content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 24, 2013


Greg Shelton
Porterville City Councilman
752 W Thurman Avenue
Porterville, CA 93257

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-13-075

Dear Mr. Shelton:

        This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the financial disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that because the Fair Political
Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
assistance (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), this letter is based on the facts presented. We
also note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act.

QUESTION

        Do you have any reporting responsibilities under the Act under the following
circumstance: You assist two long-time friends, who reside and work outside the jurisdiction of
the City of Porterville (the "City") and who have not done and do not plan on doing business
before the City, in holding a racing event by soliciting sponsors and engaging in promotional
activities for the event, including distributing "V.I.P." passes valued in total at $2,000 to $6,000.
You receive no monetary compensation but will receive free admission and possibly other
attendance benefits provided to persons such as race sponsors.

CONCLUSION

        You have no reporting responsibilities under the Act in connection with your work on
this racing event.

_________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-13-075
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are a City Councilmember for the City. Two long-time friends of yours are planning on holding a for-profit Stock Car and International Motor Contest Association race car event at a local track and have asked for your assistance.

The track is located, and both of your friends reside and work, in Tulare County but outside of the City's jurisdiction. In addition, neither of your friends have had or plan to have business before the City.

The nature of the assistance your friends have asked you to render in connection with the event is as follows: You are to be in charge of all the sponsorship and public promotion for the event, including soliciting sponsors, organizing the advertising, posting race flyers, and working at the track before and during the event. In order to increase attendance at the event, you also have been asked to distribute a number of "V.I.P." admittance passes to the event to all of the sponsors, as well as to the logistics crew and members of the public. You estimate the total value of all the passes to be distributed to be between $2,000 and $6,000, depending on how many are actually redeemed. You will receive no monetary compensation for your work in connection with the event. We assume, however, you will be provided free admission to the event and possibly receive other admittance benefits, if any, provided to persons such as race sponsors.

ANALYSIS

Among other duties, the Act imposes certain reporting requirements on individuals who, like you, serve as a member of a city council and as an elected officer. We discuss below the reporting provisions that could possibly be applicable to your question.

The Act requires campaign committees, including campaign committees controlled by candidates for elective office such as incumbent city councilmembers who maintain such committees (see Section 82007), to periodically file campaign reports detailing information on contributions received and expenditures made by the committee. (See Sections 84100 – 84511.) Section 82015(a) and Regulation 18705(a)(2) generally provide that a payment³ to a candidate is a "contribution" unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is made for other than a political purpose.

The Act also requires elected officers to report what are known as "behested payments." Specifically, Section 82015(b)(2)(B)(iii) of the Act states that if an individual or entity, at the

³ The Act defines the term "payment" quite broadly, to include "a payment, distribution, transfer, loan, deposit, gift or other rendering of money, property, services or anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible." (Section 82044.)

File No. A-13-075
Page No. 3

behest³ of an elected officer, makes monetary or in-kind donations totaling $5,000 or more in a
calendar year for a legislative, governmental or charitable purpose, that officer has to file a report
detailing those donations with his or her public agency within 30 days after reaching the $5,000
threshold and each 30 days thereafter for additional donations by the same donor. The report is
filed on FPPC Form 803, entitled "Behested Payment Report."

Finally, the Act and Commission regulations require city councilmembers to periodically
file a Statement of Economic Interests (FPPC Form 700) on which they must disclose various
financial interests held or received during the reporting period. These interests include all
sources of gifts of $50 or more regardless of jurisdiction, and all sources of income of $500 or
more if those persons either reside or do business within the city's jurisdiction or if they plan to
do business within the jurisdiction, or if they have done business within the jurisdiction in the
past two years. (Sections 87200 – 87210 and Regulations 18722 and 18723.)

Based on the facts you present, we do not believe your work on the racing event falls
under any of the Act's reporting requirements discussed above. The only "payments" you may
be receiving in connection with your work on the event is free admission and possibly other
admittance benefits provided to persons such as race sponsors. These payments are clearly in
consideration for your actual work on the for-profit racing event and are not for a political
purpose or otherwise in connection with a legislative, governmental or charitable purpose. As
such, they are neither campaign contributions nor potential "behested payments." Instead, we
would classify the payments as income, but they would not be reportable on your Statement of
Economic Interests because the income is from individuals who do not reside or work in the
City's jurisdiction and who have not done business before the City in the past two years and do
not plan to do so in the future.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: Scott Hallabrin
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:jgl

³ Under Regulation 18215.3(a), a payment is made "at the behest" of an elected officer for purposes of the
"behested payment" rule if it is "made under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with, at the request or suggestion of, or with the express, prior consent of" the elected
officer or his or her agent.

behcst3 of an elected officer, makes monetary or in-kind donations totaling $5,000 or more in a
